---
title: "Machine Learning for Economics"
subtitle: "Prediction, regularization, and causal machine learning"
---

## Overview

Machine learning entered economics by a side door --- as a set of prediction tools far better than OLS at fitting flexible, high-dimensional relationships --- and then reshaped causal inference itself. The challenge is that prediction accuracy and valid causal inference pull in different directions: the regularization that makes ML predict well also biases naive estimates of effects.

This module develops both halves. It starts with the prediction toolkit --- regularization (ridge, LASSO, elastic net) and tree ensembles (random forests, boosting) --- and the cross-validation discipline that powers them. It then turns to the genuinely new contribution: **double/debiased machine learning**, which uses ML for nuisance functions while preserving valid, root-n inference on the causal parameter through Neyman-orthogonal scores and cross-fitting; and **heterogeneous-effect estimation and policy learning**, which use ML to find who benefits and to learn optimal treatment-assignment rules. This is where the causal-inference spine of Part I meets the modern computational frontier.

## Pages in This Module

- [Regularization: Ridge, LASSO, and Elastic Net](regularization.qmd) --- Prediction, sparsity, and the bias-variance tradeoff.
- [Trees, Forests, and Boosting](trees-forests.qmd) --- Flexible nonlinear prediction from data.
- [Double and Debiased Machine Learning](double-ml.qmd) --- Using ML for nuisance functions without breaking inference.
- [Heterogeneous Effects and Policy Learning](causal-forests-policy.qmd) --- Who benefits, and what should we do?
